score_justification: The esp32c3 scored 7 out of 10 due to passing 9 out of 10 fault injection scenarios. The only critical failure was the stack_overflow_trigger scenario, which resulted in a crash instead of the expected reboot. However, all high-severity scenarios were handled, and the device did not experience any full crashes or critical memory/NVS crashes without recovery.

failed_scenarios_analysis.root_cause: inadequate stack size or improper use of recursive functions
failed_scenarios_analysis.fix_code: increase stack size or refactor recursive functions to iterative solutions

passed_scenarios_highlights.why_good: correctly calls nvs_flash_erase() to handle corruption
properly handles memory exhaustion by returning an error

memory.top_recommendation: increase stack size and implement proper memory management

nvs.top_recommendation: continue to use nvs_flash_erase() to handle corruption

matter.top_recommendation: refactor code to separate concerns and improve readability

priority_fixes: 1. [CRITICAL] Fix stack overflow trigger by increasing stack size or refactoring recursive functions
2. [HIGH] Improve code readability by separating concerns and using descriptive variable and function names
3. [MEDIUM] Organize code into separate files or modules to improve maintainability
summary: The esp32c3 demonstrated a robustness score of 7, indicating a need for improvement in memory management and code organization. While the device handled most fault injection scenarios, the stack overflow trigger scenario resulted in a critical failure. Priority fixes include addressing the stack overflow trigger and improving code readability and maintainability.
